text: The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle using traction and counter-traction techniques. A hook diathermy is used to open a plane below the taut ileocolic pedicle, dividing it. A harmonic device aids in dissection, with its convex end used for blunt dissection above the duodenum. A tonsil swab is placed anterior and inferior to the duodenum to guide further dissection.
The surgeon moves on to the transverse colon, where the cancerous area is visibly marked with tattoo ink.
The surgeon makes the omentum taut and uses a harmonic device to create a window in the omentum, which is then slowly enlarged as the dissection proceeds off the transverse colon.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally towards the swab placed in the infracolic compartment, anterior to the duodenum. The duodenum is visible below the swab as the dissection progresses to take down the hepatic flexure area.
The surgeon uses extra long graspers to aid in the dissection, allowing for manipulation and traversal over long distances within the intra-abdominal cavity.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure and proceeds laterally to mobilize the lateral part of the colon. Dense adhesions between the cecum, ascending colon, and the lateral abdominal wall are released using gentle diathermy and a harmonic device.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral side of the colon and proceeds to dissect the middle colic pedicles using a harmonic device. Hemolog clips are applied to secure the pedicles before they are divided. This step is completed prior to dividing the transverse colon distal to the tattooed site of the cancer.

The transverse colon distal to the cancer is prepared for division. An endogia stapler is used to securely divide the transverse colon at the tumor site. The entire specimen is visible after the division, and the right colon is now fully mobilized.
